description: Known as the Korean Lady's Slipper, this iconic orchid features unique pouch-like pinkish flowers. It is one of Korea's most critically endangered terrestrial orchids, representing the fragile beauty of deep mountain ecosystems.
habitat: Found in deep, humus-rich soil within shaded mountain valleys, typically at altitudes between 450m and 990m. It thrives under a deciduous forest canopy with specific light levels (canopy openness around 18%) and depends on a symbiotic relationship with mycorrhizal fungi like Lycoperdon nigrescens for nutrient absorption.
ecological_value: Acts as a biological indicator for healthy, undisturbed forest soils and complex fungal networks. Its presence signifies a mature and stable ecosystem with high micro-organism diversity.
threats: Extremely vulnerable to illegal poaching due to its high ornamental value. Additionally, its inability to easily propagate artificially and its sensitivity to climate-induced changes in soil humidity make natural recovery very slow.

description: A tiny, exquisite epiphytic orchid endemic to Jeju Island. It is characterized by its small, yellowish-green flowers with distinctive brownish spots, growing silently on the bark of aged trees.
habitat: Strictly limited to the evergreen broadleaf forests of Jeju Island. It grows as an epiphyte on the trunks and branches of old host trees in high-humidity environments, often near valleys or streams where fog is frequent.
ecological_value: A key component of the vertical biodiversity in Jeju's forest canopy, providing niche micro-habitats and resources for specialized arboreal insects.
threats: The death of ancient host trees due to forest aging and pests, combined with the island's rising average temperatures and decreasing forest humidity, poses a severe threat to its survival.

description: Prized for its elegant white flowers and powerful, sweet fragrance, this orchid is a cultural symbol of southern maritime beauty. Its thick, vibrant green leaves allow it to survive in harsh coastal winds.
habitat: Naturally found on the trunks of old trees or hanging from sea-facing cliffs on Jeju Island and the southern coastal islands. It relies on the humid sea fog and high maritime humidity to sustain its moisture needs.
ecological_value: Provides a significant nectar source for maritime pollinators and contributes to the unique genetic diversity of Korea's island flora.
threats: Almost entirely extinct in the wild due to decades of mass illegal collection for the horticultural market. Habitat fragmentation along coastal development zones further prevents the natural re-establishment of populations.

description: A rare epiphytic orchid that blooms with delicate, fleeting yellowish flowers. It is a 'ghost-like' plant that is difficult to spot unless in bloom, hidden in the dense foliage of ancient forests.
habitat: Found exclusively in the humid, shaded valleys of Jeju Island, particularly on the bark of ancient Torreya nucifera trees or other aged broadleaf species. It requires consistent air moisture and protection from direct sunlight.
ecological_value: Represents a unique evolutionary branch of epiphytic orchids in Korea and is an indicator species for ancient, undisturbed forest patches.
threats: Highly localized distribution makes it extremely vulnerable to even minor environmental changes or the loss of specific host trees. Illegal collection remains a persistent threat despite its small size.

description: Commonly known as the 'Rock Plum,' this is the world's smallest shrub. It forms dense, cushion-like mats on rocky mountain summits, blooming with pure white flowers that defy the harsh alpine wind.
habitat: Restricted to the extreme alpine tundra zone of Mt. Halla's summit (above 1,800m). It grows in rocky crevices and gravelly soil where few other plants can survive, enduring sub-zero temperatures and high-velocity winds.
ecological_value: An 'Ice-Age Relict' that has survived for thousands of years since the last glacial period. It is a crucial species for studying the botanical history and glacial migrations of East Asia.
threats: Global warming is its greatest enemy; as the summit temperatures rise, more competitive temperate plants move up, squeezing the alpine habitat. Physical trampling by hikers and soil erosion also cause direct damage.

description: A primitive fern found only on Jeju Island, often described as a 'living fossil.' It is a unique species that represents an independent evolutionary lineage within the fern family.
habitat: Grows in specific seasonal wetlands known as 'Gotjawal' on Jeju Island. It thrives in depressed areas where water collects during the rainy season and stays moist in the basaltic rock crevices during drier months.
ecological_value: Invaluable for evolutionary biology and plant systematics research. It is a 'monotypic' genus, meaning it is the only species of its kind in the entire world, all located within a small area of Jeju.
threats: The development of the Gotjawal forests for golf courses, roads, and agriculture is the primary cause of its habitat destruction. Changes in the local groundwater level also directly impact its seasonal wetland environment.

description: A terrestrial orchid with bamboo-like leaves and elegant, pale green flowers with reddish-brown spots. It is a quiet dweller of the forest floor, blending seamlessly with its surroundings.
habitat: Found in the understory of evergreen broadleaf forests on Jeju Island. It prefers moist, shaded areas with thick leaf litter that provides the necessary organic nutrients and stable soil moisture.
ecological_value: Contributes to the complex soil-root interactions of Jeju's forests and serves as a nectar source for early-summer forest insects.
threats: Illegal collection for its unique foliage and flowers is the main threat. Habitat loss due to the expansion of farmland and changes in forest composition also hinder its natural growth.

description: Known as the Tamna Orchid, this small epiphyte produces clusters of bright yellowish-orange flowers. Its presence is a sign of a vibrant, healthy subtropical forest ecosystem.
habitat: Endemic to the southern part of Jeju Island, where it grows on the bark of aged trees in valleys and evergreen forests. It requires high humidity and moderate temperatures throughout the year.
ecological_value: A flagship species for the conservation of Jeju's unique epiphytic flora and a provider of food for specialized pollinators in the valley ecosystems.
threats: Climate change and the resulting shifts in forest humidity are major long-term threats. Short-term, illegal collection remains a significant danger to the few remaining wild populations.

description: A striking orchid featuring white flowers covered in vivid purple-brown spots. The entire plant is covered in fine, soft hairs, giving it a soft, velvety appearance.
habitat: Found in high-altitude meadows and grassy slopes of the northern mountains, such as Mt. Seorak and Mt. Taebaek. It prefers cool temperatures and well-drained, nutrient-rich soil.
ecological_value: A beautiful and rare member of the high-altitude flora of the Baekdudaegan mountain range, supporting the specialized pollination networks of alpine meadows.
threats: Extremely sensitive to temperature increases; as the alpine climate warms, its habitat is shrinking. Illegal collection for its high ornamental value further decimates the remaining populations.

description: One of the most beloved orchids in East Asian culture, known for its pristine white flowers and long, elegant nectar spurs. Its leaves are thick and curved, designed to store water in maritime environments.
habitat: Naturally clings to maritime cliffs and old trees on Jeju Island and the southern coast. It survives by absorbing moisture from sea fog and rain through its specialized aerial roots.
ecological_value: A key species in the maritime ecosystem, adapted to survive where few other vascular plants can. It is a historical and genetic treasure of Korea.
threats: Nearly vanished from the wild due to centuries of collection for its fragrance and beauty. Current wild populations are incredibly rare and require strict, direct protection from poaching.

description: The Jeju variant of the Edelweiss, this plant is covered in white, woolly hairs to protect itself from intense alpine UV rays and cold. Its star-shaped bracts make it a legendary sight of Mt. Halla.
habitat: Strictly endemic to the rocky outcrops and high-altitude meadows of Mt. Halla, Jeju (above 1,500m). It grows in windy, exposed areas where it can receive maximum sunlight.
ecological_value: A flagship species for the conservation of Mt. Halla's high-altitude ecosystem and a testament to the unique botanical evolution of Jeju Island.
threats: Rising alpine temperatures are allowing more aggressive plants to invade its territory. Physical damage from hikers who stray from designated trails is also a major concern.

description: Recognized as a Natural Monument of Korea, the Winter-blooming Orchid is famous for its cold-defying spirit and noble, upright flowers. It is the pinnacle of East Asian orchid aesthetics.
habitat: Located in Seogwipo, Jeju Island, within deep, shaded valleys of evergreen broadleaf forests. It prefers moist, well-drained slopes between 200m and 300m above sea level.
ecological_value: A cultural and natural icon of Jeju Island. It plays a significant role in the winter pollination cycle, being one of the few plants to bloom in the cold season.
threats: Despite its status as a Natural Monument, illegal poaching remains the greatest threat. Habitat loss due to forest management changes and land development also poses a risk.

description: A rare, woody bean plant that produces small, elegant white flowers. It is the only species in its genus found in Korea, making it a botanical mystery and a high-value conservation target.
habitat: Restricted to a single, highly localized valley on Jeju Island. It grows in the damp, shaded understory of evergreen forests, often near stream beds.
ecological_value: Represents the northernmost limit of its genus, making it essential for research on plant migration and climate-related evolution in East Asia.
threats: Extreme rarity (less than 100 individuals in the wild) means any local disturbance?like a storm, landslide, or illegal harvest?could lead to immediate extinction.

description: An ephemeral herb that blooms with bright yellow flowers in early spring, right after the snow melts. It completes its entire life cycle in a short few weeks before the forest canopy closes.
habitat: High mountain ridges of Gangwon-do (Mt. Seorak, Mt. Odae) at altitudes above 1,000m. It grows in moist, organic-rich soil under deciduous forests.
ecological_value: A vital early-season food source for pollinators that emerge in early spring. It is a sensitive indicator of climate-induced changes in snowmelt and spring temperatures.
threats: Vulnerable to climate change; if spring arrives too early or snowmelt is inconsistent, its growth cycle is disrupted. Illegal collection by wild plant enthusiasts is also a major threat.

description: Known as the Diamond Bluebell, this plant produces stunning violet, bell-shaped flowers that hang gracefully from its stems. It is a proud endemic species that only grows on the Korean Peninsula.
habitat: Found on cool, north-facing slopes of high mountains in central and eastern Korea (Mt. Geumgang, Mt. Seorak). It prefers altitudes between 580m and 1,400m in moist, loamy soil.
ecological_value: A 'monotypic' genus endemic to Korea, making it a critical genetic resource. It supports local bee populations who are the primary pollinators of its bell-shaped flowers.
threats: Habitat destruction due to the construction of mountain facilities and trails. Climate change is also pushing its cool-climate habitat further up the mountains.

description: A majestic lily with upward-facing, vibrant orange-red flowers covered in dark spots. Its name comes from the wing-like ridges on its stems, and it stands as a fiery symbol of alpine meadows.
habitat: Grows in sunny, high-altitude alpine meadows and grassy slopes of northern mountain ranges. It prefers well-drained soil and high-light environments.
ecological_value: A major nectar source for high-altitude butterflies and a key species for the biodiversity of subalpine grasslands.
threats: Illegal collection for its beautiful flowers is the primary threat. Over-grazing by wild animals and habitat loss from trail development also contribute to its decline.

description: A fascinating parasitic plant that lacks chlorophyll and lives entirely off the nutrients of its host. It produces spikes of pale purple flowers that emerge from the soil like ghostly fingers.
habitat: Endemic to Korea, found near riverbanks and sandy gravel areas. It is specifically parasitic on the roots of Artemisia (Wormwood) species in regions like Mt. Baegyang and Jeju Island.
ecological_value: Represents a unique parasitic relationship within the Korean flora. Its presence indicates a stable and specific host-parasite ecosystem.
threats: Habitat loss from riverbank development and the removal of its host plants (Artemisia) are the biggest dangers. Its specialized lifestyle makes it impossible to relocate once its habitat is disturbed.

description: The true Korean Edelweiss, this plant is famous for its fuzzy, star-shaped flowers that look like they are made of white felt. It is a symbol of courage and purity in Korean mountain culture.
habitat: Found on rocky cliffs and sunny, high-altitude slopes of mountains like Mt. Seorak and Mt. Odae. It is well-adapted to harsh winds and intense sunlight.
ecological_value: A flagship species for alpine conservation. Its woolly hairs are an incredible adaptation for temperature regulation and UV protection.
threats: Illegal collection by hikers as souvenirs has historically devastated many populations. Habitat encroachment by shrubs and other plants due to climate change is a growing concern.

description: A unique iris that blooms in the late afternoon and withers by morning. Its fan-like leaves and pale violet flowers make it a mysterious beauty of the western islands.
habitat: Strictly limited to the sunny, fertile coastal slopes of Daecheong and Baengnyeong Islands. It thrives in open, windy areas near the sea.
ecological_value: An 'Evening Bloomer' that has a specific relationship with nocturnal or crepuscular pollinators. Its narrow distribution makes it a high-priority maritime conservation species.
threats: Habitat destruction for military and coastal facilities. Additionally, over-grazing by livestock on the islands and its own specialized reproductive cycle make it vulnerable.

description: The 'Prickly Water Lily' is a giant aquatic plant with leaves that can grow up to 2 meters wide, entirely covered in sharp thorns. Its mysterious purple flowers emerge from the water like a dragon's eye.
habitat: Grows in old, stagnant ponds and wetlands in southern Korea (e.g., Upo Wetland). It requires deep, nutrient-rich mud and stable water levels for its massive leaves to expand.
ecological_value: The largest freshwater plant in Korea. It provides essential habitat and shade for various aquatic insects, fish, and water birds.
threats: The primary threat is wetland drainage and the transformation of old ponds into agricultural or industrial land. Water pollution also prevents its sensitive seeds from germinating.

description: A beautiful iris featuring white petals with a distinct, bright yellow pattern at the center. It is an endemic species that adds a splash of color to the spring forest floor.
habitat: Found in the understory of deciduous forests and shaded forest edges in central and eastern Korea, most famously on Mt. Odae. It prefers moist, loamy soil at altitudes between 300m and 1,500m.
ecological_value: An endemic species that contributes to the floral diversity of the Baekdudaegan mountain range and supports spring pollination networks.
threats: Illegal harvesting for home gardens and habitat loss from mountain trail widening and forest development projects.

description: A localized variant of the Aster, this plant produces elegant pale purple flowers. It is a resilient survivor that thrives on the rocky limestone banks of the Namhan River.
habitat: Restricted to the rocky limestone riverbanks and gravel bars of the Namhan River in Danyang and Yeongwol. It is adapted to survive seasonal flooding and poor soil conditions.
ecological_value: A specialized limestone-habitat plant that helps stabilize riverbank soil and provides resources for river-valley insects.
threats: Habitat destruction due to river development projects (like dam and weir construction) and the invasion of exotic plants like Humulus japonicus that smother its growth.

description: A stunning wildflower with deep scarlet, fringed petals that resemble the wings of a swallow. It is a rare gem of the highland wetlands, glowing like fire amidst the green grass.
habitat: Grows in high-altitude wetlands and moist meadows in Gangwon-do. It requires consistently wet soil and full sunlight to produce its vibrant red blooms.
ecological_value: An indicator species for the health of highland wetlands. It provides a unique nectar source for specialized highland butterflies.
threats: Wetland drying due to climate change and agricultural runoff. It is also a target for illegal collection because of its uniquely beautiful and rare red flowers.

description: A dwarf iris known for its deep violet flowers that bloom close to the ground. Its name 'Gaksi' (meaning 'bride') reflects its small, modest, and traditional beauty.
habitat: Found in sunny forest edges and grassy slopes throughout the country. It prefers well-drained soil and moderate sunlight, often appearing in early spring.
ecological_value: Adds to the genetic and aesthetic diversity of Korea's spring flora and supports early-season pollinators.
threats: Though more widely distributed than some, its habitats are frequently cleared for development. It is also often dug up for use in rock gardens.

description: Often called the 'White Forsythia,' this is a world-class rare plant. It is a monotypic genus, meaning this is the only species of its kind in the world, and it is native only to Korea.
habitat: Grows on rocky, limestone-rich slopes in specific regions like Goesan and Yeongdong. It prefers areas with good drainage and plenty of spring sunlight.
ecological_value: A botanical treasure of global importance. It is one of the first plants to bloom in spring, providing critical early nectar for bees.
threats: Naturally small and fragmented populations. Historically, it was heavily poached for its uniqueness, and current populations are threatened by habitat changes and invasive species.

description: The world's most famous flower, symbolizing love and beauty. In EcoGo, it represents the potential for every person to cultivate a beautiful green space.
habitat: Grows in a wide variety of environments, but prefers sunny areas with well-drained, fertile soil. It is a staple in urban gardens and parks worldwide.
ecological_value: Supports urban biodiversity by providing nectar for a wide range of bees and butterflies. It also improves air quality in city environments.
threats: Commonly affected by fungal diseases like black spot and pests like aphids. While not endangered, wild species of roses require protection from habitat loss.

description: A spring-blooming bulb known for its vibrant colors and cup-shaped flowers. It is a symbol of perfect love and the joy of a new season.
habitat: Thrives in sunny spots with well-drained soil. They are widely planted in temperate regions during the autumn for a spring display.
ecological_value: An early-season nectar source for bees emerging from winter. They enhance the aesthetic value of urban green spaces, boosting human well-being.
threats: Mainly affected by bulb rot and squirrels who may dig them up. They require careful water management to prevent fungal growth.

description: A towering plant known for its massive yellow flower heads that follow the sun. It is a symbol of loyalty, longevity, and positive energy.
habitat: Requires full sun and deep, fertile soil to support its height. It is highly adaptable and can grow in various fields and gardens.
ecological_value: A powerhouse for pollinators, attracting numerous bees and birds. Its seeds provide a high-energy food source for wildlife during the winter.
threats: Vulnerable to high winds due to its height. While common, they can be affected by drought and specific pests like the sunflower moth.

description: An autumn favorite, known for its incredible variety of shapes and colors. It represents longevity and is deeply rooted in East Asian culture.
habitat: Prefers full sun and rich, moist soil. They are hardy plants that bloom when most other flowers have faded for the year.
ecological_value: Provides a crucial late-season food source for pollinators before they hibernate. They are also known for their air-purifying properties.
threats: Sensitive to waterlogged soil which can cause root rot. They are generally resilient but can be targeted by spider mites and aphids.

description: A flower of pure white beauty and intense fragrance. It is often used in ceremonies and represents rebirth and purity.
habitat: Grows best in sunny or partially shaded spots with 'cool feet' (shaded roots). They require well-drained soil to prevent bulb damage.
ecological_value: Attracts nocturnal pollinators with its strong evening fragrance. They are a significant part of the floral ecosystem in both wild and cultivated settings.
threats: Highly toxic to cats and can be damaged by lily beetles. They require protection from extreme moisture during their dormant period.

description: Known for its ruffled petals and spicy fragrance, the carnation is a symbol of gratitude and affection, especially on Mother's Day.
habitat: Prefers cool climates and well-drained, slightly alkaline soil. They are popular in greenhouses and home gardens.
ecological_value: Provides nectar for various butterflies and adds to the structural diversity of garden flower beds.
threats: Can be affected by wilt and rust diseases. While common, they require consistent attention to soil pH and water levels.

description: The ephemeral symbol of spring, blooming in clouds of pink and white. It represents the fleeting nature of life and the beauty of a fresh start.
habitat: Grows in temperate climates, often planted along streets and in parks. They prefer sunny spots and well-drained, rich soil.
ecological_value: A critical spring food source for thousands of bees and insects. They also provide nesting sites for birds in urban areas.
threats: Vulnerable to various wood-boring insects and fungal diseases. Urban trees also face stress from pollution and soil compaction.

description: One of the first signs of spring in Korea, this shrub produces bright yellow, bell-shaped flowers that cover its long, arching branches.
habitat: Highly resilient and grows in most soils, provided they are not too wet. It is a common sight in gardens, hillsides, and city streets.
ecological_value: An essential early nectar source. Its dense growth provides excellent cover and nesting habitat for small songbirds.
threats: Very few natural threats due to its hardiness, but it can be affected by leaf spot or gall if not properly pruned and cared for.

description: A beloved flower of the Korean mountains, blooming in shades of purple and pink before its leaves appear. It is a symbol of nostalgic spring days.
habitat: Grows on sunny or partially shaded mountain slopes and hillsides across the peninsula. It prefers acidic soil and moderate moisture.
ecological_value: A core species of the Korean forest understory, providing food for early insects and contributing to the seasonal rhythm of the ecosystem.
threats: Habitat loss due to mountain development and the encroachment of larger trees that shade them out. They are also sensitive to extreme drought.

description: A bright yellow or white spring flower with a central trumpet. It symbolizes rebirth and is a herald of the end of winter.
habitat: Grows from bulbs in sunny or lightly shaded meadows and gardens. They are very hardy and often return year after year.
ecological_value: One of the earliest sources of pollen for bees. Their toxic alkaloids prevent them from being eaten by deer and rabbits, allowing them to thrive in the wild.
threats: Bulb rot from overly wet soil and the 'narcissus bulb fly.' While not endangered, they require proper drainage to survive the dormant season.

description: Known for its dense spikes of fragrant, bell-shaped flowers. It comes in a wide range of colors and is a popular choice for spring indoor and outdoor gardens.
habitat: Prefers sunny locations with fertile, well-drained soil. They are typically planted as bulbs in the autumn.
ecological_value: Attracts early spring bees with its strong fragrance. They provide a high-density nectar source in a small area.
threats: The bulbs are sensitive to rot and can be damaged by excessive frost if not buried deep enough. They also decline in vigor over several years.

description: A charming flower with 'face-like' patterns on its petals. It is incredibly hardy and can even survive a light frost, making it a garden favorite.
habitat: Thrives in cool weather and partial sun. They are often planted in early spring or autumn to avoid the intense summer heat.
ecological_value: Provides a food source for caterpillars of certain fritillary butterflies. They are excellent for pollinators in the shoulder seasons.
threats: Slugs and snails are their primary enemies. They also struggle in high summer temperatures, often wilting or going dormant.

description: A popular summer flower known for its trumpet-shaped blooms and endless color combinations. It is a staple of hanging baskets and window boxes.
habitat: Requires full sun and consistent moisture. They are versatile and can grow in containers or as ground cover in gardens.
ecological_value: Attracts hummingbirds and long-tongued moths with their tubular flowers. They provide a long-lasting nectar source throughout the summer.
threats: Susceptible to various viruses and pests like aphids. They can also become 'leggy' and stop blooming if not regularly deadheaded.

description: Known for its waxy leaves and constant blooms, the begonia is a reliable and easy-to-grow plant for both shade and sun.
habitat: Depends on the variety, but many prefer partial shade and moist, well-drained soil. They are often used as bedding plants in urban landscapes.
ecological_value: Provides a consistent, albeit low-level, nectar source for pollinators in shaded areas where fewer other flowers may grow.
threats: Very sensitive to frost and can be affected by powdery mildew if there is poor air circulation.

description: Also known as the Pot Marigold, it features bright orange or yellow flowers and is often used in traditional medicines and balms.
habitat: Grows easily in sunny spots with average soil. It is a hardy annual that often self-seeds and returns the following year.
ecological_value: A fantastic plant for beneficial insects like ladybugs and lacewings, helping with natural pest control in gardens.
threats: Generally pest-free, but can be affected by powdery mildew in humid weather. It is not endangered but its wild ancestors require protection.

description: Famous for its soothing scent and purple flower spikes. It is a Mediterranean native that has become a global symbol of relaxation and healing.
habitat: Needs full sun and very well-drained, somewhat poor soil. It is highly drought-tolerant once established.
ecological_value: A top-tier pollinator plant, attracting an incredible variety of bees and butterflies. Its fragrance also helps repel unwanted garden pests.
threats: Root rot from overly wet soil is the biggest killer of lavender. It also requires regular pruning to prevent it from becoming woody.

description: Known for its delicate, trumpet-shaped flowers that line one side of the stem and its sweet, peppery fragrance.
habitat: Prefers sunny locations with well-drained soil. They are often grown as winter or spring bulbs in mild climates.
ecological_value: The strong scent is highly attractive to bees and other pollinators. They contribute to the aromatic diversity of spring gardens.
threats: Sensitive to extreme cold and requires careful watering. Like most bulbs, they can suffer from rot if kept in soggy conditions.

description: A cloud-like plant with thousands of tiny white flowers. It is the perfect filler in bouquets and symbolizes everlasting love and innocence.
habitat: Prefers full sun and alkaline, well-drained soil. It is a hardy perennial that can grow quite large in the right conditions.
ecological_value: Provides a massive amount of small-scale nectar for tiny pollinators like wasps and small bees that other flowers may not cater to.
threats: In some regions, it can become invasive if not managed. In the garden, it is relatively pest-free but needs space for air circulation.

description: The quintessential simple flower, with a yellow center and white petals. It symbolizes innocence, purity, and a loyal love.
habitat: Grows in lawns, meadows, and gardens. It is highly adaptable and can survive in most temperate environments with moderate sun.
ecological_value: Provides a steady nectar source for bees and small insects. Its flat shape makes it an easy landing platform for many pollinators.
threats: Often treated as a weed in formal lawns and targeted by herbicides. While very common, their wild populations are declining in some areas.

description: Though often seen as a weed, the dandelion is a vital survival plant. Its bright yellow flowers and 'blowball' seeds are a classic part of any childhood.
habitat: Extremely hardy and can grow almost anywhere, from cracks in the sidewalk to open fields. It prefers sunny spots but is not picky about soil.
ecological_value: A critical first food for bees in the early spring when nothing else is blooming. Its deep taproots also help aerate the soil and pull up nutrients.
threats: The main threat is the widespread use of herbicides in urban and suburban areas. Without dandelions, many pollinators would struggle in early spring.

description: An autumn favorite with feathery foliage and daisy-like flowers. It symbolizes order and peace, swaying gracefully in the autumn breeze.
habitat: Grows easily in sunny spots with average soil. They are often planted in large fields to create a sea of pink, white, and purple in the autumn.
ecological_value: A fantastic plant for attracting butterflies, especially during their autumn migration. They are also popular with bees and lacewings.
threats: Generally very healthy, but can be affected by powdery mildew if planted too densely. They are not endangered and are widely cultivated.
